summary: The article supports a bounded overhead mechanism for why context files raise cost, while most performance claims remain comparative and explicitly limited rather than mechanistically explained.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Adding repository-level context files generally reduced task success relative to no repository context while increasing inference cost by more than 20%.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence establishes the empirical tradeoff, but not a full causal explanation for why success declines; it supports only the bounded claim that added context can impose overhead.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Developer-provided context files performed better than LLM-generated context files and produced only a modest average gain over no context.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports the comparative outcome, but mechanism remains uncertain; it does not show which properties of developer-written files caused the small advantage, and the gain was not universal because Claude Code did not improve over no context.

bounded_claims.claim_text: LLM-generated context files slightly hurt agent performance on average and increased cost, though they became helpful when other repository documentation was removed.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a context-dependent result rather than a general rule; these files underperformed in the main setting but improved outcomes when other documentation was absent, so mechanism is unresolved and usefulness is conditional on the surrounding documentation regime.
